prop 218.note: (auto cite-check, web) Prop 218 exists and is correctly identified with Article XIII D, §6(b)'s cost-of-service and parcel-proportionality requirements (§6(b)(1), (b)(3)); the snippets' description of a cross-subsidization concern under §6(b)(3) and of courts applying independent-judgment/no-deference review to rate decisions is consistent with the constitutional text and with case law such as Silicon Valley Taxpayers Ass'n v. Santa Clara County Open Space Authority (2008) 44 Cal.4th 431. The passing reference to Article X, §2/Water Code §375 is used as a separate, competing conservation-doctrine authority, not as an alternate label for Prop 218, so it doesn't affect this cite's accuracy. Canonical: Proposition 218 (Nov. 1996), codified at Cal. Const. art. XIII C & art. XIII D; substantive fee requirements at art. XIII D, § 6(b) (incl. § 6(b)(1) cost-of-service cap and § 6(b)(3) proportionality requirement). Sources: https://law.justia.com/constitution/california/article-xiii-d/section-6/; https://lao.ca.gov/1996/120196_prop_218/understanding_prop218_1296.html; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1996_California_Proposition_218

prop 26.note: (auto cite-check, web) Proposition 26 (2010) is correctly identified as adding Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e), which defines "tax" and sets out seven enumerated exceptions for fees/charges, including the requirement that costs allocated to a payor bear a "fair or reasonable relationship" to the payor's burden or benefit. The documents' use of "Prop 26's reasonable relationship fee-exceptions test" as distinct from Prop 218/Art. XIII D §6(b)'s cost-of-service proportionality test for property-related fees is a standard, accurate characterization consistent with official text and practitioner guides (League of CA Cities, CSDA, Meyers Nave). Canonical: Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e) (as added/amended by Proposition 26, approved Nov. 2, 2010). Sources: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displayText.xhtml?lawCode=CONS&article=XIII+C; https://meyersnave.com/wp-content/uploads/LOCC-Implementation-Guide-Prop-26-and-218.pdf; https://www.gcsd.org/files/4da2c072f/CSDA+Guide-Proposition+26.pdf

235 cal.app.4th 1493.note: (auto cite-check, web) Case exists with this exact reporter cite and year (4th Dist., confirmed by FindLaw, AALRR, Allen Matkins, Maven's Notebook, and others). Holding matches usage: the court analyzed a tiered, conservation-oriented water-rate structure under Cal. Const. art. XIII D §6(b)'s cost-of-service framework, holding tiers are permissible only if they correspond to actual cost of service rather than being set purely to incentivize conservation — this supports [dialectic]'s claim that the case adjudicated a tiered conservation rate "entirely within the §6(b) framework," and [tetlock]'s framing as an open question (categorical exclusion vs. cost-tracking requirement) is an accurate, appropriately cautious characterization of the unresolved nuance. Canonical: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn., Inc. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493. Sources: https://caselaw.findlaw.com/court/ca-court-of-appeal/1698183.html; https://www.aalrr.com/newsroom-alerts-4059; https://mavensnotebook.com/2015/07/23/water-rate-making-after-capistrano-taxpayers-association-v-san-juan-capistrano/

39 cal.4th 205.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case exists and the citation form (39 Cal.4th 205, decided July 24, 2006) is correct. Its core holding — that charges for ongoing metered/volumetric domestic water delivery to a parcel are "fees" or "charges" imposed as an incident of property ownership for a "property related service" subject to Article XIII D (and that Art. XIII C §3 initiative power lets voters reduce, but not impose preapproval on, such rates) — is accurately reflected in the [delphi] and [dialectic] uses (which cite it generally to Art. XIII D §6). The [tetlock] snippet's specific pincite to "§6(b)(4)" is imprecise: Bighorn's classification holding is not tied to that subdivision (which concerns actual-use/immediate-availability), and secondary sources confirm the opinion does not analyze 6(b)(3)/(b)(4) in detail — that proportionality/availability analysis is more associated with later cases like Capistrano. This is a minor subsection-level imprecision in one usage, not a misattribution of the case or its central, well-established holding. Canonical: Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205. Sources: https://www.courtlistener.com/opinion/2631763/bighorn-desert-view-water-agency-v-verjil/; https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/bighorn-desert-etc-v-verjil-33651; https://www.waterlawjournal.com/upgrading-conservation-pricing-proposition-218-smart-meters-and-the-step-beyond-tiered-rates/

375.note: (auto cite-check, web) Section 375 exists (Div. 1, Ch. 3.5, Water Conservation Programs) and authorizes public water entities, by ordinance/resolution after hearing and findings, to adopt conservation programs, require water-saving devices, and "encourage water conservation through rate structure design." This supports the documents' use of it as the statutory hook for a conservation-rate/conservation-mandate defense in the drought-surcharge Prop 218 hypothetical; the citation form (Cal. Water Code § 375) is correct. Canonical: Cal. Water Code § 375. Sources: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=WAT&sectionNum=375; https://law.justia.com/codes/california/2019/code-wat/division-1/chapter-3-5/section-375/; https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/water-code/wat-sect-375.html

220 cal.app.4th 586.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case exists and the cite (reporter/year/court) is correct; Griffith actually held the augmentation charge IS a property-related fee under Art. XIII D (imposed as an incident of ownership), exempt only from Prop 218's voter-approval requirement via the water-service carve-out, and separately upheld the agency's revenue/cost-allocation methodology (supporting the [dialectic] use). The [tetlock] snippet inverts this, calling Griffith authority for a 'not property-related/extraction not ownership' argument — that rationale actually belongs to City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191, which expressly disapproved Griffith on this point. Canonical: Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586. Sources: https://caselaw.findlaw.com/ca-court-of-appeal/1646971.html; https://blog.aklandlaw.com/2013/10/articles/exactions-impact-fees-service-charges-and-property-taxes/court-affirms-groundwater-augmentation-charge-exempt-from-proposition-218-as-a-water-service-charge/; https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/city-san-buenaventura-v-united-water-conservation-district-34548

1021.5.note: (auto cite-check, web) Section exists and the statutory text confirmed via leginfo/FindLaw matches: it authorizes a court, on motion, to award attorney's fees to a successful party in an action enforcing an important right affecting the public interest, where (a) a significant public benefit is conferred, (b) the necessity/financial burden of private enforcement makes an award appropriate, and (c) fees shouldn't come out of any recovery — expressly applicable to actions against public entities. The documents' generic use — flagging § 1021.5 "private attorney general" fee-shifting exposure for a public agency in Prop 218 ratepayer litigation — is a standard, well-recognized application of this doctrine (Prop 218/tax-and-fee challenges against public agencies are a common fee-shifting context under this section); no specific case holding was claimed that required separate verification. Canonical: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1021.5. Sources: https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?sectionNum=1021.5.&lawCode=CCP; https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/code-of-civil-procedure/ccp-sect-1021-5/

city of palmdale v. palmdale water dist.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case exists and the citation form is correct, but the documents' usage inverts the holding. The Court of Appeal actually struck down Palmdale Water District's water-budget-based tiered rate structure, holding the District failed to carry its Prop. 218 burden of showing that tier pricing corresponded to the actual cost of service (conservation/rate-stability goals alone can't justify disproportionate tiers). The [dialectic] snippet's claim that individualized water-budget tiers were "upheld" to "strengthen the parcel-attributable-cost nexus" gets the outcome backwards; [premortem]'s parallel characterization (also flagged "verify") is likewise wrong, though its hedge at least signals uncertainty. [delphi]'s bare citation without a holding claim is not itself mischaracterized. Canonical: City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926. Sources: https://kmtg.com/news/legal-alerts/water-districts-new-rate-structure-failed-to-comply-with-requirements-of-proposition-218/; https://www.aalrr.com/newsroom-alerts-4059; https://pioneerlawgroup.net/water-district-failed-show-rate-structure-complied-proposition-218-requirements-property-related-fees/

198 cal.app.4th 926.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case exists and the cite form is correct, but the documents get the holding backwards. The Court of Appeal reversed judgment for the water district, invalidating its water-budget-based tiered rate structure because the district failed to show any cost-of-service basis for the tier disparities, as Proposition 218 requires proportionality to actual cost of service (conservation rationale alone was insufficient). The [dialectic] and [premortem] snippets describe budget-based tiers as 'upheld' by Palmdale — the opposite of what the court held; [delphi] merely lists the cite without characterizing it, which is fine. Canonical: City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926. Sources: https://www.aalrr.com/newsroom-alerts-4059; https://kmtg.com/news/legal-alerts/water-districts-new-rate-structure-failed-to-comply-with-requirements-of-proposition-218/; https://pioneerlawgroup.net/water-district-failed-show-rate-structure-complied-proposition-218-requirements-property-related-fees/

7 cal.5th 372.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case is real and the citation form is correct, but Plantier's actual holding is narrow: a Prop. 218 challenge to an agency's *method* of allocating a fee (as opposed to the rate itself) need not exhaust administrative remedies via the § 6(a) protest hearing, because that hearing is an inadequate forum to evaluate an established allocation methodology. It is not a burden-of-proof/evidentiary case. The [dialectic] snippet's use — that Plantier "confirms" a facial/methodological challenge "can proceed without parcel-by-parcel individualized proof from the plaintiff" as part of a "Bighorn/Plantier line" requiring agencies to affirmatively prove cost-of-service compliance — conflates Plantier's exhaustion-of-remedies holding with an evidentiary burden-shifting rule the case doesn't address (that burden-shifting rule actually derives from Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(5) itself, and Bighorn addressed a different issue — initiative power over rates, not proof burdens). The [steelman] snippet's hedged reference to Plantier "on procedural vehicle, which may affect available remedy" is a fair characterization; the [delphi] mention is a bare citation-count listing with no substantive claim attached. Canonical: Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372. Sources: https://law.justia.com/cases/california/supreme-court/2019/s243360.html; https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/plantier-v-ramona-mun-water-dist-34614; https://www.counties.org/post/plantier-v-ramona-municipal-water-district-s243360

view water agency v. verjil.note: (auto cite-check, web) Confirmed: real CA Supreme Court decision (July 24, 2006), correctly cited at 39 Cal.4th 205. The Court held that domestic water delivery through a pipeline is a "property related service" and that charges for it — including volumetric/metered charges — are "fees" or "charges" under Article XIII D, §6, notwithstanding usage-based billing; this squarely supports the snippets' use of Bighorn to foreclose a "not property-related" threshold argument and to establish that metered retail water delivery charges are property-related fees subject to §6(b). Canonical: Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, 46 Cal.Rptr.3d 73, 138 P.3d 220. Sources: https://www.courtlistener.com/opinion/2631763/bighorn-desert-view-water-agency-v-verjil/; https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/bighorn-desert-etc-v-verjil-33651; https://www.lawpipe.com/California/Bighorn-Desert_View_Water_Agency_v_Verjil.html

capistrano taxpayers assn. v. city of sa.note: (auto cite-check, web) Both authorities exist and cite forms are correct. Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held that tiered/inclining water rates must be justified by actual cost-of-service differences at each tier, not merely by usage budgets or conservation goals — this directly supports the documents' use of it to flag the district's "conservation-incentive pricing" rationale as the doctrinal defect. Bighorn (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 correctly stands for the proposition that metered, parcel-based water delivery charges are property-related fees subject to Article XIII D, consistent with its use as the "paradigm case for Art. XIII D coverage." Canonical: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn., Inc. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 (4th Dist.); see also Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205. Sources: https://caselaw.findlaw.com/court/ca-court-of-appeal/1698183.html; https://www.aalrr.com/newsroom-alerts-4059; https://www.courtlistener.com/opinion/2631763/bighorn-desert-view-water-agency-v-verjil/

of san buenaventura v. united water cons.note: (auto cite-check, web) Confirmed: the California Supreme Court held that a groundwater pumping/extraction charge imposed on well operators (extractors, not retail property owners) is not a "property-related fee" under Art. XIII D and instead is evaluated under Art. XIII C (Prop. 26)'s reasonable-relationship-to-burden/benefit standard, remanding for that analysis. This matches the dialectic and naive_fable snippets' use (distinguishing it as an extraction charge on pumpers, not a retail property-related fee). The tetlock snippet's use — invoking it for a general "reasonable relationship/no-mathematical-precision" latitude for tiered retail rates under Art. XIII D — is a looser, more generalized extension of the case's actual holding (which addressed threshold applicability of XIII D vs. XIII C to a different kind of charge), but is not a misattribution of the case or cite. Canonical: City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191. Sources: https://law.justia.com/cases/california/supreme-court/2017/s226036.html; https://www.downeybrand.com/legal-alerts/california-supreme-court-confirms-certain-groundwater-pumping-charges-are-outside-scope-of-prop-218/; https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/city-san-buenaventura-v-united-water-conservation-district-34548

223 cal.app.4th 892.note: (auto cite-check, web) The case exists and the reporter cite (223 Cal.App.4th 892) is correct; it upheld an IID cost-of-service-based tiered water rate structure and an omnibus Prop. 218 protest procedure covering multiple customer classes, consistent with the dialectic/naive_fable use for aggregate rather than parcel-level cost matching. However, the steelman snippet's parenthetical calling it a "Prop 26 context" case is wrong — Morgan is exclusively a Proposition 218 (Article XIII D, §6) case with no Prop. 26 issue, so that characterization misattributes the case's legal framework. Canonical: Morgan v. Imperial Irrigation Dist. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 892 (Fourth Dist., Div. One; consol. Nos. D060146, D061087; orig. filed Jan. 17, 2014, certified for pub. Feb. 4, 2014). Sources: https://caselaw.findlaw.com/ca-court-of-appeal/1656571.html; https://somachlaw.com/policy-alert/unpublished-opinion-from-the-fourth-district-addresses-proposition-218-fee-protest-procedures-for-water-rate-increases-involving-multiple-classes-of-customers/; https://www.calattorneysfees.com/2014/02/private-attorney-general-morgan-v-imperial-irrigation-district-now-published.html
